table_of_contents.title: Clarifications from last time and lecture framing
RNN hidden state, unrolling, and vanilla equations
Computational graphs, shared weights, and BPTT
Character-level language modeling and interpretable cells
RNN tradeoffs and computer-vision sequence applications
Multilayer RNNs and notation for variants
Gradient flow problems and LSTM design

units.name: Dropout, normalization, and training logistics recap
units.description: Clarifies dropout test-time scaling, the normalization versus initialization discussion, and experiment tracking before the RNN lecture begins.
units.summary: Dropout was clarified as convention-dependent: most libraries use p as probability of dropping a unit, while the slide used p as probability of keeping a unit active [ts=36s]. The key rule is to make test-time output match expected train-time output; dropping 25% of activations means scaling by 0.75 at test time [ts=52s]. The LayerNorm example used a 2D quadrant classification task and showed normalization can reduce the harm from bad initialization, but cannot replace good Kaiming-style initialization [ts=91s] [ts=125s]. Normalization can also hurt when exact coordinates matter because mean/std normalization removes some spatial information [ts=145s]. The recap also situates non-recurrent training topics: setup choices, transfer learning, training dynamics, validation tuning, and tools such as Weights & Biases [ts=180s] [ts=240s].

key_points.text: At test time, scale dropout activations so expected test output equals expected training output.
LayerNorm can help bad initialization but a performance gap remains without good initialization.
Normalization may hurt if exact spatial coordinates are required.
Weights & Biases is shown as a tool for comparing hyperparameter runs and validation performance.

units.name: Sequence modeling and RNN motivation
units.description: Introduces variable-length sequence modeling and the standard input-output task regimes.
units.summary: The lecture shifts from fixed-size inputs/outputs to sequence modeling, where inputs and/or outputs may have variable length [ts=289s]. RNNs and variants are introduced as the main simple neural sequence models used before transformers, with key ideas still appearing in state-space models such as Mamba [ts=303s] [ts=313s]. The instructor distinguishes one-to-many image captioning, many-to-one video classification, and many-to-many sequence mappings [ts=351s] [ts=377s] [ts=401s]. For explaining RNN mechanics, the lecture focuses on the aligned many-to-many case where each input step has a corresponding output, such as frame-level video classification [ts=422s].

key_points.text: Sequence modeling handles variable-length inputs or outputs rather than only fixed-size vectors.
RNN ideas remain relevant in modern state-space models such as Mamba.
One-to-many maps an image to a variable-length caption; many-to-one maps video frames to one class.
The lecture initially focuses on aligned many-to-many RNNs.

units.name: RNN mechanics: hidden state, unrolling, and vanilla equations
units.description: Defines recurrent hidden state, unrolled RNN diagrams, output projection, and the vanilla tanh RNN.
units.summary: An RNN has an input sequence x, output sequence y, and a recurrent internal hidden state that updates while the sequence is processed [ts=448s] [ts=470s]. The current hidden state depends on both x_t and h_{t-1}; unrolling makes this dependency and the gradient order explicit [ts=479s] [ts=501s]. The hidden-state recurrence applies the same function and same parameters W at every time step [ts=533s] [ts=571s]. A separate output function, often using W_hy, maps the hidden dimension to the output dimension and learns a transformation [ts=591s] [ts=618s]. Vanilla RNNs commonly use tanh for h_t because it is bounded, zero-centered, and can represent positive and negative values; the simplest output is a matrix multiply from h_t to y_t [ts=792s] [ts=823s].

key_points.text: RNNs maintain a hidden state updated as each input arrives.
Unrolling shows h_t depends on x_t and h_{t-1}.
The same recurrent function and parameters W are reused at every time step.
W_hy converts hidden state to output dimension through a learned projection.
Vanilla RNNs often use tanh because it is bounded and zero-centered.

units.name: Hand-built RNN for detecting repeated 1s
units.description: A worked example manually designs an RNN that detects consecutive ones in a binary sequence.
units.summary: The concrete example builds an RNN by hand so the forward pass and matrix roles are visible [ts=840s]. The toy many-to-many task takes a binary sequence and outputs 1 only when two 1s occur consecutively [ts=868s] [ts=895s]. To solve it, the hidden state must store the previous input and current x; the example uses a three-dimensional hidden vector for current, previous, and a constant 1, initialized as h_0=(0,0,1) [ts=917s] [ts=934s] [ts=958s]. ReLU is used for simple arithmetic [ts=988s]. W_xh maps x=0 to [0,0,0]^T and x=1 to [1,0,0]^T, W_hh copies the former current value into the previous slot and preserves the constant, and the output computes ReLU(current+previous-1) so only two consecutive ones produce 1 [ts=1069s] [ts=1131s] [ts=1232s].

key_points.text: Toy task: output 1 when the binary input has two consecutive 1s.
The hidden state should capture previous input and current x.
The example hidden vector stores current, previous, and constant 1, initialized as (0,0,1).
W_xh writes the current input value into the hidden state.
The output uses ReLU(current + previous - 1).

units.name: Shared-weight computational graphs and BPTT
units.description: Explains how gradients are computed in unrolled RNNs and why truncated BPTT is used.
units.summary: The lecture then explains gradient computation when an RNN has multiple time steps and possibly losses at each step [ts=1321s] [ts=1446s]. In the computational graph, every time step uses the same weight matrices; for intuition, gradients can be computed as if each time step had a copy of W, then summed because W is actually shared [ts=1453s] [ts=1523s] [ts=1551s]. Many-to-many models may sum per-step losses, while many-to-one models may use the final hidden state or pooled hidden states [ts=1475s] [ts=1578s]. Backpropagation through time is this gradient computation through repeated applications of the same weights, but long sequences require storing activations and gradients at many steps, creating GPU memory pressure [ts=1667s] [ts=1691s]. Truncated BPTT fixes a time window, passes the final hidden state forward as the next window’s initialization, and stops gradients across window boundaries [ts=1704s] [ts=1740s] [ts=1751s]. Single-output RNNs still backpropagate through earlier hidden states via upstream gradients from the final loss [ts=1782s] [ts=1819s].

key_points.text: RNN computational graphs reuse the same weights at every time step.
Many-to-many losses can be summed across time steps.
Gradients from different time steps are summed into one update for the shared W.
Backpropagation through time computes gradients through repeated time-step uses of the same weights.
Truncated BPTT carries hidden states forward but cuts gradients between fixed windows.

units.name: Character-level language modeling and interpretable cells
units.description: Shows character-level RNN language modeling, embeddings, sampling, and interpretable hidden activations.
units.summary: A character-level language model is presented as a practical RNN example and an early ancestor of current language models [ts=2194s] [ts=2208s]. Characters can be one-hot encoded, the RNN updates hidden layers from the current input and previous hidden state, and the output predicts the next character via scores or softmax, making the task time-step-wise classification [ts=2221s] [ts=2242s] [ts=2280s]. At test time, characters are sampled one at a time and fed back into the model [ts=2294s]. Practical models use embedding layers: a learned matrix row is selected as the input vector, which optimizes better than passing sparse one-hot vectors directly [ts=2320s] [ts=2414s]. The lecture cites min-char-rnn.py as a 112-line implementation that generates increasingly coherent Shakespeare or code as training progresses [ts=2383s] [ts=2462s] [ts=2505s]. Greedy decoding always picks the maximum-probability output, so practical generation samples from the softmax distribution or uses beam search [ts=2626s] [ts=2677s]. Some RNN hidden cells become interpretable, tracking quotes, line length, if-statements, comments, or code depth [ts=2741s] [ts=2862s].

key_points.text: Character-level RNN language modeling predicts the next character at each time step.
Generated characters are fed back into the RNN at test time.
Embedding layers select a learned vector row instead of using raw one-hot inputs.
Greedy decoding can repeat the same output; sampling or beam search gives alternatives.
RNN cells can track interpretable features such as quotes, if-statements, and code depth.

units.name: RNN tradeoffs and vision-language applications
units.description: Covers practical pros/cons of RNNs and historical computer-vision sequence uses.
units.summary: RNN advantages include processing arbitrary-length sequences, keeping model size fixed as length grows, and reusing the same weights at every time step [ts=2899s] [ts=2948s]. In theory, hidden states can carry information from far back, but in practice recurrence is hard to batch and fixed-size hidden states lose information over long sequences [ts=2926s] [ts=2982s] [ts=3032s]. In image captioning, a CNN or visual encoder represents the image, often via a pretrained model’s second-to-last layer, and an RNN decoder combines that visual information with previous generated text until an end token appears [ts=3056s] [ts=3076s] [ts=3107s] [ts=3161s]. The instructor notes captioning failures caused by dataset co-occurrence bias and hallucination of common objects or actions [ts=3191s] [ts=3229s]. Other historical applications include VQA, visual dialog, and visual language navigation [ts=3291s] [ts=3364s] [ts=3382s].

key_points.text: RNNs can process arbitrary-length sequences with a fixed-size model.
Sequential dependence makes RNN training hard to batch and scale.
Fixed-size hidden states may lose information from far back in long sequences.
Image captioning can use a pretrained CNN representation as input to an RNN decoder.
Captioning models can hallucinate objects/actions due to dataset co-occurrence bias.

units.name: Multilayer RNNs and compact notation
units.description: Explains stacked recurrent layers and shorthand matrix notation before introducing LSTMs.
units.summary: Practical RNNs are often multilayer, analogous to stacked CNN or fully connected layers [ts=3408s] [ts=3422s]. In a multilayer RNN, each layer’s hidden state depends on that same layer’s previous time step, while upper layers take lower-layer outputs as their inputs, forming a time-by-depth grid [ts=3430s] [ts=3463s]. Computing later high-layer states requires many earlier hidden states, which makes training involved and inefficient [ts=3483s]. The lecture introduces LSTMs as a key 1990s RNN variant designed to alleviate vanilla RNN disadvantages [ts=3502s] [ts=3521s]. It also reviews the vanilla update with W_hh and W_xh and explains that authors often combine them into one larger W by stacking vectors, although the conceptual blocks remain distinct [ts=3537s] [ts=3562s] [ts=3571s]. Weights are shared through time within a layer, but each stacked layer has its own weight set [ts=3641s].

key_points.text: Multilayer RNNs stack recurrent layers over both depth and time.
Within a layer, h_t depends on that layer’s h_{t-1}; upper layers use lower-layer outputs.
Computing late high-layer states requires many earlier hidden states.
W_hh and W_xh can be notated as one larger W over stacked vectors.
Weights are shared through time within a layer, but different layers have separate weights.

units.name: Gradient flow problems and LSTM architecture
units.description: Derives vanishing/exploding gradients in vanilla RNNs and explains the LSTM fix at a high level.
units.summary: Backpropagating through a vanilla RNN repeatedly multiplies by W_hh and the derivative of tanh [ts=3664s] [ts=3704s]. Since tanh’s derivative is at most 1 and usually below 1, it contributes to vanishing gradients [ts=3780s] [ts=3795s]. Even without the nonlinearity, repeated multiplication by W_hh can explode gradients when singular values are large or vanish them when singular values are small [ts=3814s] [ts=3839s]. Exploding gradients can be clipped, but vanishing gradients are a major reason long vanilla RNNs are hard to use [ts=3846s] [ts=3857s]. LSTMs address this by using input, forget, and output gates plus a cell-state pathway that makes it easier to pass information without repeatedly applying tanh [ts=3890s] [ts=3930s] [ts=3970s]. LSTMs do not guarantee a solution, but they make long-term dependency learning significantly easier and work well empirically [ts=4035s] [ts=4065s].

key_points.text: Vanilla RNN gradients repeatedly multiply by W_hh and tanh derivatives.
The derivative of tanh is at most 1 and often less, causing vanishing gradients.
Large singular values can explode gradients; small singular values can vanish them.
Gradient clipping mitigates exploding gradients but not the core vanishing-gradient problem.
LSTMs use gates and a cell-state path to selectively write, forget, output, and preserve information.

units.name: LSTM skip paths, state-space models, and wrap-up
units.description: Connects LSTM information flow to ResNets and modern RNN-like state-space models.
units.summary: The instructor connects LSTM direct information paths to ResNet skip connections: both copy or add values across parts of the computation to improve information and gradient flow [ts=4098s] [ts=4111s]. The analogy differs in axis because ResNets skip across layer depth, while LSTMs help across long time sequences [ts=4132s] [ts=4142s]. The final topic is the resurgence of RNN-like ideas through modern state-space models, motivated by unlimited context and linear scaling with sequence length [ts=4152s] [ts=4174s] [ts=4197s]. RWKV and Mamba are cited as examples aiming for linear-time sequence modeling rather than transformer-style quadratic scaling [ts=4224s] [ts=4233s]. The wrap-up states that RNNs offer flexible architecture design, vanilla RNNs are simple but weak, variants such as LSTMs and Mamba selectively pass information, gradients can vanish or explode, BPTT is often needed, and better sequence-reasoning architectures remain active research [ts=4258s] [ts=4276s] [ts=4289s].

key_points.text: LSTM direct paths are analogous to ResNet skip connections for improving information flow.
RNN-like state-space models are motivated by unlimited context and linear sequence-length scaling.
RWKV and Mamba are examples of modern linear-time sequence models.
Vanilla RNNs are simple but weak; variants selectively pass information and address gradient-flow issues.

unit_kp_map_local.coverage_rationale: Dropout scaling and normalization/init caveats are the central recap topics [ts=52s].
The section defines sequence modeling task regimes and examples [ts=351s].
State-space models such as Mamba are previewed as modern relatives [ts=313s].
Hidden-state recurrence is defined as the core RNN mechanism [ts=470s].
The vanilla tanh update and output projection are introduced [ts=792s].
The repeated-1 example hand-designs the hidden state and weights [ts=917s].
The example instantiates recurrent hidden-state equations with W_xh and W_hh [ts=1024s].
The unrolled graph reuses the same W at every time step [ts=1453s].
BPTT is named and described as summing time-step gradients [ts=1691s].
Truncated BPTT uses fixed windows and stops gradients across windows [ts=1704s].
Character-level next-token prediction, embeddings, and sampling are discussed [ts=2194s].
Interpretable cells show hidden states tracking sequence features [ts=2741s].
RNN tradeoffs and vision-language applications dominate this section [ts=2899s].
Image captioning uses autoregressive token generation with an end token [ts=3161s].
Multilayer RNNs are explained as a depth-by-time grid [ts=3422s].
LSTMs are introduced as a variant addressing vanilla RNN weaknesses [ts=3502s].
The vanishing/exploding gradient derivation is the main focus [ts=3780s].
LSTM gates and the cell pathway are explained as the fix [ts=3890s].
RWKV and Mamba are tied to linear-time sequence modeling [ts=4224s].
The LSTM path is compared to ResNet skip connections [ts=4098s].

section_flags.rationale: Worked matrix example for repeated-1 detection.
BPTT and truncated BPTT are central training mechanics.
Language-modeling and sampling examples are useful retrieval targets.
Tradeoffs and CV applications support conceptual breadth.
Gradient-flow derivation and LSTM gates are critical.
Synthesis with modern state-space models and summary.
